inlineContent.text: [!NOTE]
TTS is not yet implemented for all formats.
Text-to-speech can be used to read aloud a publication using a synthetic voice. The Readium toolkit ships with a TTS implementation based on the native
, but it is opened for extension if you want to use a different TTS engine.
engine
– a TTS engine takes an utterance and transforms it into audio using a synthetic voice
tokenizer
- algorithm splitting the publication text content into individual utterances, usually by sentences
utterance
- a single piece of text played by a TTS engine, such as a sentence
voice
– a synthetic voice is used by a TTS engine to speak a text using rules pertaining to the voice’s language and region
To read a publication, you need to create an instance of
. It orchestrates the rendition of a publication by iterating through its content, splitting it into individual utterances using a
, then using a
to read them aloud. Not all publications can be read using TTS, therefore the constructor returns an optional object. You can also check whether a publication can be played beforehand using
Then, begin the playback from a given starting
. When missing, the playback will start from the beginning of the publication.
You should now hear the TTS engine speak the utterances from the beginning.
provides the APIs necessary to control the playback from the app:
- stops the playback ; requires start to be called again
- interrupts the playback temporarily
- resumes the playback where it was paused
- toggles the pause
- skips to the previous utterance
- skips to the next utterance
Look at
in the Test App for an example of a view calling these APIs.
The
should be the single source of truth to represent the playback state in your user interface. You can observe the state with
to keep your user interface synchronized with the playback. The possible states are:
when idle and waiting for a call to
when interrupted while playing the associated utterance.
when speaking the associated utterance. This state is updated repeatedly while the utterance is spoken, updating the
value with the portion of utterance being played (usually the current word).
When pairing the
with a
, you can use the
and
properties to highlight spoken utterances and turn pages automatically.
[!WARNING]
The way the synthesizer is configured is expected to change with the introduction of the new Settings API. Expect some breaking changes when updating.
The
offers some options to configure the TTS engine. Note that the support of each configuration option depends on the TTS engine used.
Update the configuration by setting it directly. The configuration is not applied right away but for the next utterance.
The language used by the synthesizer is important, as it determines which TTS voices are used and the rules to tokenize the publication text content.
By default,
will use any language explicitly set on a text element (e.g. with
in HTML) and fall back on the global language declared in the publication manifest. You can override the fallback language with
which is useful when the publication language is incorrect or missing.
The
setting can be used to change the synthetic voice used by the engine. To get the available list, use
To restore a user-selected voice, persist the unique voice identifier returned by

Users do not expect to see all available voices at all time, as they depend on the selected language. You can group the voices by their language and filter them by the selected language using the following snippet.

content.text: Synchronizing the TTS with a Navigator

inlineContent.text: While
is completely independent from
and can be used to play a publication in the background, most apps prefer to render the publication while it is being read aloud. The
core model is used as a means to synchronize the synthesizer with the navigator.

inlineContent.text: You can use the same technique as described above to automatically synchronize the
with the played utterance, using
However, this will not turn pages mid-utterance, which can be annoying when speaking a long sentence spanning two pages. To address this, you can use the
associated value of the
state instead. It is updated regularly while speaking each word of an utterance. Note that jumping to the
locator for every word can severely impact performances. To alleviate this, you can throttle the observer.

code: public func publicationSpeechSynthesizer(_ synthesizer: PublicationSpeechSynthesizer, stateDidChange synthesizerState: PublicationSpeechSynthesizer.State) {

inlineContent.text: By default, the
will split the publication text into sentences to create the utterances. You can customize this for finer or coarser utterances using a different tokenizer.
For example, this will speak the content word-by-word:
For completely custom tokenizing or to improve the existing tokenizers, you can implement your own
can be used with any TTS engine, provided they implement the
interface. Take a look at
for an example implementation.

abstract.text: To open a publication with Readium, you need to instantiate a couple of components: an
You can use the Readium Swift toolkit to download and read publications that are protected with the
All migration steps necessary in reading apps to upgrade to major versions of the Swift Readium toolkit will be documented in this file.
The Readium Swift Toolkit is a toolkit for ebooks, audiobooks, and comics written in Swift & Kotlin.
Learn about the architecture, configuration, and usage of the Readium Navigator.
Some publications declare their accessibility features and limitations as metadata which broadly mirror the
The Readium Swift toolkit enables you to develop reading apps for iOS and iPadOS. It provides built-in support for multiple publication formats such as EPUB, PDF, audiobooks, and comics.
